5:["$","li","1",{"className":"flex items-center gap-1","children":[["$","$L4",null,{"iconNode":[["path",{"d":"m9 18 6-6-6-6","key":"mthhwq"}]],"className":"lucide-chevron-right text-stone-300 shrink-0","size":13}],["$","$L3",null,{"href":"/blog","className":"hover:text-orange-500 transition-colors","children":"Blog"}]]}]
6:["$","li","2",{"className":"flex items-center gap-1","children":[["$","$L4",null,{"iconNode":"$5:props:children:0:props:iconNode","className":"lucide-chevron-right text-stone-300 shrink-0","size":13}],["$","$L3",null,{"href":"/blog?category=Productivity","className":"hover:text-orange-500 transition-colors","children":"Productivity"}]]}]
7:["$","li","3",{"className":"flex items-center gap-1","children":[["$","$L4",null,{"iconNode":"$5:props:children:0:props:iconNode","className":"lucide-chevron-right text-stone-300 shrink-0","size":13}],["$","span",null,{"className":"text-stone-600 font-medium line-clamp-1","children":"Getting Things Done (GTD): A Practical Guide to the Method"}]]}]
8:["$","div",null,{"className":"bg-white dark:bg-stone-900 rounded-2xl p-6 sm:p-8 mt-4 shadow-lg border border-stone-100 dark:border-stone-800 mb-10","children":[["$","h1",null,{"className":"text-2xl sm:text-3xl lg:text-4xl font-bold text-stone-900 dark:text-stone-100 leading-tight mb-5","style":{"fontFamily":"'Playfair Display', Georgia, serif"},"children":"Getting Things Done (GTD): A Practical Guide to the Method"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"flex flex-wrap items-center gap-4 text-sm text-stone-400","children":[["$","$L3",null,{"href":"/authors/sara-osei","className":"flex items-center gap-2 group","children":[["$","img",null,{"src":"https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1438761681033-6461ffad8d80?w=400&h=400&fit=crop&auto=format&q=80","alt":"Sara Osei","className":"w-8 h-8 rounded-full object-cover object-top"}],["$","div",null,{"children":[["$","p",null,{"className":"font-semibold text-stone-700 dark:text-stone-200 text-sm group-hover:text-orange-600 transition-colors","children":"Sara Osei"}],["$","p",null,{"className":"text-xs text-stone-400","children":"Productivity Researcher"}]]}]]}],["$","span",null,{"className":"flex items-center gap-1","children":[["$","$L4",null,{"iconNode":[["circle",{"cx":"12","cy":"12","r":"10","key":"1mglay"}],["path",{"d":"M12 6v6l4 2","key":"mmk7yg"}]],"className":"lucide-clock","size":13}]," ","9 min read"]}],["$","span",null,{"children":"March 17, 2026"}]]}]]}]
9:["$","div",null,{"className":"grid grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-10 pb-16","children":[["$","article",null,{"className":"lg:col-span-2","children":[["$","p",null,{"className":"text-xl text-stone-600 leading-relaxed mb-10 font-medium pl-4 border-l-4 border-orange-500","children":"David Allen's Getting Things Done is one of the most influential productivity systems ever written. This guide covers the five steps, how to implement them without becoming obsessed with the system, and what the research says about why it works."}],["$","div",null,{"className":"prose","children":[["$","h2","0",{"id":"what-gtd-is-and-is-not","children":"What GTD Is (and Is Not)"}],["$","p","1",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Getting Things Done (GTD) is a productivity methodology developed by David Allen and described in his 2001 book of the same name. It is one of the most widely adopted and most widely misunderstood productivity systems."}}],["$","p","2",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"What GTD is: a system for capturing everything that has your attention, clarifying what those things mean for you, organising the results, reviewing regularly, and engaging with the work that matters."}}],["$","p","3",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"What GTD is not: a time management system. Allen explicitly frames GTD as an attention management system. The goal is to free your mind from the burden of remembering and tracking commitments so that your full attention is available for the work in front of you."}}],["$","h2","4",{"id":"the-core-insight","children":"The Core Insight"}],["$","p","5",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Your brain is excellent at generating ideas and solving problems. It is poor at storing and tracking commitments reliably. When you rely on your brain as a storage system — keeping mental track of everything you need to do — you create background anxiety and cognitive load that impairs the very thinking you need to do your work."}}],["$","p","6",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"GTD's solution: a trusted external system that captures and tracks everything, so your brain can stop trying to remember and focus entirely on the current task."}}],["$","h2","7",{"id":"the-five-steps","children":"The Five Steps"}],["$","h3","8",{"id":"1-capture","children":"1. Capture"}],["$","p","9",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Collect everything that has your attention — tasks, ideas, commitments, projects, reference material — into capture tools. This means everything: the email you need to respond to, the book recommendation from a colleague, the errand you keep forgetting, the project idea that occurred to you in the shower."}}],["$","p","10",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Capture tools can be: a physical inbox, a notebook, a digital app, a voice memo. The tool matters less than the habit of capturing immediately rather than leaving things in your head."}}],["$","h3","11",{"id":"2-clarify","children":"2. Clarify"}],["$","p","12",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Process what you captured. For each item, ask: what is this? Is it actionable?"}}],["$","ul","13",{"children":[["$","li","0",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"If no action is needed: delete it, file it for reference, or incubate it (a \"someday/maybe\" list for things you might want to do one day but are not committed to)"}}],["$","li","1",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"If action is needed: define the next physical action (not a vague intention, but a specific action you could do right now) and either do it (if under two minutes), delegate it, or defer it"}}]]}],"$Lc","$Ld","$Le","$Lf","$L10","$L11","$L12","$L13","$L14","$L15","$L16","$L17","$L18","$L19","$L1a"]}],"$L1b","$L1c","$L1d"]}],"$L1e"]}]
a:["$","script","script-0",{"src":"/_next/static/chunks/0wu484j-81s3l.js","async":true}]
b:["$","$L1f",null,{"children":["$","$20",null,{"name":"Next.MetadataOutlet","children":"$@21"}]}]
c:["$","h3","14",{"id":"3-organise","children":"3. Organise"}]
d:["$","p","15",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Place the results in the right place:"}}]
e:["$","ul","16",{"children":[["$","li","0",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<strong>Next Actions list</strong>: the specific next step for every active project, organised by context (at computer, on phone, at desk, errands)"}}],["$","li","1",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<strong>Projects list</strong>: everything requiring more than one action step to complete"}}],["$","li","2",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<strong>Waiting For list</strong>: things delegated to others"}}],["$","li","3",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<strong>Calendar</strong>: only hard commitments with specific times and dates"}}],["$","li","4",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<strong>Someday/Maybe list</strong>: ideas for the future"}}]]}]
f:["$","h3","17",{"id":"4-reflect","children":"4. Reflect"}]
10:["$","p","18",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Review the system regularly. The Weekly Review is the cornerstone: every week, go through all your lists, review upcoming calendar, process any outstanding captures, and ensure every project has a clear next action defined."}}]
11:["$","p","19",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Without the Weekly Review, the system decays. Lists become stale, captures accumulate unprocessed, and trust in the system — which is what frees your mind — erodes."}}]
12:["$","h3","20",{"id":"5-engage","children":"5. Engage"}]
13:["$","p","21",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Choose what to do. With a trusted system, the question \"what should I be doing right now?\" is answered by consulting your next actions list, your calendar, and your current context and energy level."}}]
14:["$","p","22",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"You make a clear choice rather than defaulting to whatever is most urgent or most comfortable."}}]
15:["$","h2","23",{"id":"why-it-works-the-science","children":"Why It Works (The Science)"}]
16:["$","p","24",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"The Zeigarnik Effect explains much of GTD's benefit. Incomplete tasks occupy working memory until they are either completed or captured in a trusted system. Allen's system is effective because it provides the trusted external system that allows the brain to release the unfinished task, reducing background cognitive load."}}]
17:["$","p","25",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Research by Masicampo and Baumeister confirms this: people who made a specific plan for an unfinished task showed significantly less intrusive thinking about it than those who did not — even though the task remained incomplete."}}]
18:["$","h2","26",{"id":"the-gtd-trap","children":"The GTD Trap"}]
19:["$","p","27",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"The most common failure mode: over-investing in the system itself. Creating elaborate folder structures, testing every GTD app, watching productivity YouTube. The system exists to support work, not to replace it."}}]
1a:["$","p","28",{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"Start minimal: one capture tool, one next actions list, one projects list. Weekly review every Friday. The full system can be added as the habit matures."}}]
1b:["$","div",null,{"className":"mt-10 pt-8 border-t border-stone-200 dark:border-stone-800 flex flex-wrap items-center gap-3","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"text-sm font-medium 1c:["$","$L22",null,{"title":"Getting Things Done (GTD): A Practical Guide to the Method","slug":"getting-things-done-guide"}]
1d:["$","div",null,{"className":"print:hidden","children":["$","$L23",null,{}]}]

25:["$","$L4",null,{"iconNode":[["path",{"d":"M7 7h10v10","key":"1tivn9"}],["path",{"d":"M7 17 17 7","key":"1vkiza"}]],"className":"lucide-arrow-up-right","size":14}]
